stem: A 7-year-old with acute asthma is in the ED. Which findings require immediate intervention? Select all that apply.
opts: SpO2 88% on room air
Respiratory rate 22/min
Nasal flaring and suprasternal retractions
Child unable to speak in full sentences
Mild expiratory wheeze on deep breath only
Heart rate 68/min
rat: SpO2 less than 90%, accessory muscle use (retractions), and inability to speak full sentences indicate severe distress. RR 22 and HR 68 are within normal range; mild wheeze on deep breath alone is not emergent.
optRationales: SpO2 88% is critically low — below the 90% threshold — and indicates severe hypoxia requiring immediate intervention.
RR 22/min is within normal range for a 7-year-old (18–30) and does not require immediate intervention.
Nasal flaring and suprasternal retractions indicate significant increased work of breathing — an emergency sign.
Inability to speak in full sentences indicates severe respiratory distress and requires immediate intervention.
Mild wheeze only on deep breath is a mild finding and not immediately emergent on its own.
HR 68/min is within normal range for a school-age child and is not an emergency finding.

stem: A child with asthma is receiving nebulized albuterol. Albuterol works primarily by:
opts: Reducing airway inflammation via corticosteroid pathway
Relaxing bronchial smooth muscle through beta-2 adrenergic stimulation
Thinning secretions through mucolytic action
Decreasing mast cell degranulation
rat: Albuterol is a short-acting beta-2 agonist (SABA) that relaxes bronchial smooth muscle causing bronchodilation. It does not reduce inflammation or directly affect mast cells.
optRationales: Albuterol is a beta-2 adrenergic agonist that directly relaxes bronchial smooth muscle, causing bronchodilation — this is its primary mechanism.
Corticosteroids (not albuterol) reduce airway inflammation. Albuterol does not work via the corticosteroid pathway.
Mucolytics thin secretions. Albuterol has no mucolytic action.
Albuterol does not inhibit mast cell degranulation. Cromolyn sodium works on mast cells.

stem: Parents of a newly diagnosed 4-month-old with CF ask about nutrition. Which statement indicates understanding?
opts: We will restrict fat to protect the pancreas
We will give pancreatic enzymes with each feeding
Normal growth is expected without extra calories
We need to limit sodium in warm weather
rat: CF causes exocrine pancreatic insufficiency; enzymes must be given with each feeding. Caloric needs are 120-150% of normal; fat is NOT restricted. Sodium supplementation is needed due to sweat losses.
optRationales: Restricting fat is incorrect — CF patients need 120–150% of normal calories, and fat restriction is contraindicated.
Pancreatic enzyme replacement with each feeding is correct — CF causes exocrine pancreatic insufficiency preventing nutrient absorption.
Normal growth without extra calories is incorrect — CF increases metabolic demands significantly.
Limiting sodium in warm weather is incorrect — CF patients lose excessive sodium in sweat and actually need sodium supplementation.

stem: The nurse is assessing a 10-year-old with cystic fibrosis. Which findings are expected? Select all that apply.
opts: Barrel chest from air trapping
Digital clubbing
Steatorrhea and foul-smelling stools
Hypotension in warm weather from sodium loss
Normal sweat chloride test result
Productive cough with thick purulent sputum
rat: CF hallmarks: barrel chest (air trapping), clubbing (chronic hypoxia), steatorrhea (enzyme insufficiency), sodium loss in sweat, and productive thick cough. Sweat chloride greater than 60 mEq/L is diagnostic - it is NOT normal.
optRationales: Barrel chest from air trapping is expected in CF due to chronic obstructive physiology.
Digital clubbing is expected — it reflects chronic tissue hypoxia from persistent lung disease.
Steatorrhea occurs because pancreatic enzyme deficiency prevents fat digestion and absorption.
Sodium loss via sweat can cause hyponatremia and dehydration — hypotension risk is real in warm weather.
Normal sweat chloride is incorrect — a value greater than 60 mEq/L is diagnostic for CF.
Productive cough with thick purulent sputum is a hallmark of CF lung disease.

stem: A 6-year-old is 4 hours post-tonsillectomy, restless, and swallowing frequently.
actions: Offer ice chips and reassure
Notify the surgeon immediately
Administer PRN antiemetic
Position upright and keep NPO
rat: Frequent swallowing = blood dripping down the posterior pharynx = hemorrhage. Notify surgeon immediately; keep NPO and upright. Monitor SpO2 and swallowing/HR (tachycardia is an early hemorrhage sign).

stem: Child A: barky cough, low-grade fever, inspiratory stridor. Child B: drooling, tripod position, high fever, muffled voice. Which statement guides priority care correctly?
opts: Both need immediate throat cultures
Child A may get cool mist; Child B's throat must NOT be examined
Child B should be placed supine for assessment
Child A needs bedside laryngoscopy
rat: Child A has croup - cool/humidified air and racemic epinephrine are appropriate. Child B has epiglottitis - examining the throat or placing supine can cause complete obstruction. Airway management for epiglottitis is done in OR only.
optRationales: Child A with barky cough and stridor has croup — cool mist is appropriate. Child B with drooling and tripod positioning has epiglottitis — throat examination is absolutely contraindicated as it can cause complete obstruction.
Decreasing SIMV is irrelevant to either child's presentation.
Placing Child B supine is dangerous — it can cause the swollen epiglottis to obstruct the airway completely.
Bedside laryngoscopy for Child A is not indicated for croup management.

stem: The nurse is preparing to care for a child with suspected epiglottitis. Which actions are appropriate? Select all that apply.
opts: Keep the child in a position of comfort (usually sitting upright)
Attempt to visualize the throat with a tongue depressor
Prepare for emergency intubation at the bedside
Keep a calm, quiet environment to minimize agitation
Administer nebulized racemic epinephrine immediately
rat: Epiglottitis management: allow position of comfort, prepare for emergent airway, keep the child calm. Do NOT examine the throat. Racemic epinephrine is for croup, not epiglottitis.
optRationales: Keeping the child in a position of comfort (usually sitting upright) allows them to maintain the best airway patency.
Examining the throat with a tongue depressor in suspected epiglottitis can trigger complete airway obstruction — absolutely contraindicated.
Emergency intubation equipment must be at the bedside — complete airway obstruction can occur suddenly.
Keeping the environment calm and quiet minimizes agitation and oxygen demand — critical in epiglottitis.
Racemic epinephrine is used for croup (subglottic), not epiglottitis (supraglottic).

stem: Which findings suggest bacterial pneumonia rather than bronchitis in a 3-year-old? Select all that apply.
opts: Focal crackles on auscultation
Diffuse bilateral expiratory wheeze
Lobar consolidation on chest X-ray
WBC 18,000 with left shift
Barky seal-like cough
Dullness to percussion over RLL
rat: Bacterial pneumonia: focal crackles, lobar consolidation, elevated WBC with left shift, dullness to percussion. Diffuse bilateral wheeze = bronchitis/bronchiolitis. Barky cough = croup.
optRationales: Focal crackles suggest consolidation — a hallmark of pneumonia rather than diffuse bronchitis.
Diffuse bilateral wheeze is characteristic of bronchitis or bronchiolitis — not pneumonia.
Lobar consolidation on CXR is the defining finding of bacterial pneumonia.
Elevated WBC with left shift indicates bacterial infection consistent with pneumonia.
Barky seal-like cough is classic for croup, not pneumonia.
Dullness to percussion over the RLL indicates consolidation — consistent with pneumonia.

stem: A 2-year-old with RSV bronchiolitis is admitted. Priority intervention?
opts: IV antibiotics immediately
Supportive care with humidified oxygen and hydration
Chest physiotherapy every 2 hours
Corticosteroids to reduce airway inflammation
rat: RSV bronchiolitis is viral. Treatment is supportive: humidified oxygen, hydration, suctioning. Corticosteroids and chest PT have not shown benefit in bronchiolitis.
optRationales: IV antibiotics are not indicated for viral RSV bronchiolitis.
Supportive care with humidified oxygen and hydration is the evidence-based treatment for RSV bronchiolitis.
Chest physiotherapy has not shown benefit in bronchiolitis and is not recommended.
Corticosteroids have not shown benefit in bronchiolitis — unlike asthma, bronchiolitis is not steroid-responsive.

stem: A newborn chokes immediately with feeds, turns cyanotic, and the NG tube won't advance past 10 cm. Priority nursing action?
opts: Attempt feeding with thickened formula
Position flat and suction
Elevate HOB 30-45 degrees, keep NPO, notify physician
Give oxygen and start chest PT
rat: Failed NG advancement plus choking with feeds = TEF/esophageal atresia. Elevate HOB to reduce aspiration, keep NPO, and notify for emergent surgical consult.
optRationales: Thickened formula would be contraindicated — the obstruction prevents passage to the stomach.
Positioning flat and suctioning does not address the structural problem and increases aspiration risk.
Elevating HOB reduces aspiration risk, keeping NPO prevents further aspiration, and provider notification enables emergent surgical consultation — all correct.
Chest PT has no role in TEF — the problem is structural, not secretion-related.

stem: The nurse is assessing a 4-year-old in respiratory distress. Which findings indicate increased work of breathing? Select all that apply.
opts: Substernal retractions
Nasal flaring
Grunting on expiration
SpO2 97% on room air
Intercostal retractions
Tripod positioning
rat: Signs of increased work of breathing: retractions (substernal, intercostal), nasal flaring, expiratory grunting, and tripod positioning. SpO2 97% on room air is reassuring.
optRationales: Substernal retractions indicate the diaphragm is working harder than normal — a sign of increased WOB.
Nasal flaring indicates the accessory nasal muscles are being recruited to open the airway.
Grunting on expiration is caused by partial glottic closure to maintain positive end-expiratory pressure — a sign of significant respiratory distress.
SpO2 97% on room air is reassuring and does not indicate increased WOB.
Intercostal retractions indicate negative intrathoracic pressure drawing in the chest wall — a sign of increased WOB.
Tripod positioning maximizes diaphragmatic excursion — a sign of significant respiratory distress.

stem: The nurse notes digital clubbing in a 7-year-old with a known cardiac defect. Which statement about clubbing is accurate?
opts: Clubbing is a normal variant in children under 8
Clubbing indicates acute hypoxemia requiring immediate oxygen
Clubbing results from chronic tissue hypoxia and altered peripheral blood flow
Clubbing reverses within hours once oxygenation is restored
rat: Clubbing reflects chronic hypoxia causing periosteal proliferation. It is NOT normal, not acute, and takes months to reverse. Seen in CF, cyanotic CHD, and chronic pulmonary disease.
optRationales: Clubbing is not a normal variant at any age — it always warrants investigation.
Clubbing reflects chronic, not acute, hypoxia. It develops over months to years.
Clubbing results from chronic tissue hypoxia causing periosteal proliferation and altered peripheral blood flow — the correct mechanism.
Reversal of clubbing takes months, not hours, once oxygenation is restored.

stem: Post-op care for a 3-month-old after cleft lip repair. Indicate whether each action is Indicated, Contraindicated, or Non-essential.
rows: Remove restraints periodically for ROM and skin checks
Cleanse suture line gently after feedings
Use syringe or cup feeding instead of nipple
rat: Elbow restraints prevent touching the repair; remove periodically for ROM/skin. Prone risks suture disruption. Suture cleansing prevents infection. Cup/syringe feeding avoids suction pressure on the repair.

stem: A 10-year-old with severe RLQ pain suddenly reports pain relief after 6 hours. Temp 39.2C, abdomen rigid. Priority interpretation?
opts: Appendicitis ruled out
Peritonitis from likely appendiceal rupture
Normal appendicitis progression
Discharge with analgesics
rat: Sudden pain relief plus fever plus board-like rigidity = appendiceal perforation with peritonitis - a surgical emergency.
optRationales: Sudden pain relief in appendicitis is a red flag — it often means the appendix has ruptured, temporarily releasing the pressure causing pain.
Sudden pain relief followed by fever and board-like rigidity is the classic presentation of appendiceal rupture with peritonitis — a surgical emergency.
This is not normal progression — sudden pain relief is a warning sign, not reassurance.
Discharging with analgesics would be dangerous given the signs of perforation and peritonitis.

stem: A 9-month-old has intermittent inconsolable crying and lethargy. Suspected intussusception. Which findings support this? Select all that apply.
opts: Currant-jelly stools (blood and mucus)
Bilious vomiting
Sausage-shaped abdominal mass
Sudden episodic drawing up of knees to abdomen
Diffuse rebound tenderness throughout abdomen
rat: Classic intussusception: episodic colicky pain (knees to abdomen), currant-jelly stools, bilious vomiting, and a palpable sausage-shaped mass. Diffuse rebound = peritonitis, not uncomplicated intussusception.
optRationales: Currant-jelly stools (blood and mucus) result from mucosal ischemia from bowel telescoping — a classic intussusception sign.
Bilious vomiting occurs due to intestinal obstruction proximal to the bile duct entry.
A sausage-shaped abdominal mass is the telescoped bowel segment — palpable in the RUQ.
Episodic drawing up of knees represents colicky pain from the telescoping bowel — classic presentation.
Diffuse rebound tenderness throughout the abdomen suggests peritonitis, which is a complication — not the typical uncomplicated presentation.

stem: A confirmed intussusception in a 10-month-old is being treated. The nurse anticipates which initial treatment?
opts: Emergency open laparotomy
Air or barium enema reduction under fluoroscopy
IV antibiotics for 48 hours then surgery
Nasogastric decompression alone
rat: First-line treatment without perforation is air or hydrostatic enema under fluoroscopy, which reduces the telescoping bowel non-surgically in ~80% of cases.
optRationales: Emergency open laparotomy is reserved for cases where enema reduction fails or perforation is suspected.
Air or hydrostatic enema reduction under fluoroscopy is first-line treatment with about 80% success rate in uncomplicated intussusception.
IV antibiotics alone without reduction does not address the mechanical obstruction.
Nasogastric decompression alone does not reduce the telescoped bowel segment.

stem: A 4-week-old has non-bilious projectile vomiting after every feeding, appears hungry afterward, and has a visible gastric wave. Labs: Na 130, K 3.0, Cl 88, pH 7.52. What does this pattern represent?
opts: Metabolic acidosis with hypokalemia
Respiratory alkalosis
Hypochloremic hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis
Normal newborn electrolytes
rat: Pyloric stenosis causes repeated loss of HCl leading to hypochloremic hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis (elevated pH, low Cl, low K).
optRationales: Metabolic acidosis would show low pH with elevated bicarb compensation — not the pattern here.
Respiratory alkalosis involves low PaCO2 from hyperventilation — not seen here.
Repeated vomiting of HCl from pyloric stenosis causes hypochloremic hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis — elevated pH, low chloride, low potassium. This matches the labs.
These electrolytes are significantly abnormal and not consistent with normal newborn values.

stem: Nurse is teaching parents of a 2-month-old with GERD. Which instructions should be included? Select all that apply.
opts: Thicken formula with rice cereal per provider direction
Position infant supine for sleep per AAP guidelines
Burp the infant frequently during feedings
Increase feeding volume to reduce frequency
Keep infant upright 20-30 minutes after feedings
rat: Thickened feeds reduce regurgitation. Supine sleep remains AAP recommendation (SIDS risk outweighs reflux risk). Frequent burping and upright positioning reduce reflux. Increasing volume worsens GERD.
optRationales: Thickening formula reduces regurgitation frequency and volume — appropriate for GERD management.
Despite reflux concerns, supine sleep remains the AAP recommendation — SIDS risk outweighs reflux risk in infants.
Frequent burping reduces swallowed air and gastric distension — reduces reflux episodes.
Increasing feeding volume worsens GERD by increasing gastric distension and reflux pressure.
Keeping the infant upright 20–30 minutes post-feeding uses gravity to reduce reflux.

stem: Parents of a 7-year-old, toilet-trained since age 3, report daily soiling. Nurse suspects functional encopresis. Best explanation?
opts: The child is regressing - needs psychiatric referral first
Chronic constipation caused rectal distension; child can no longer feel the urge
This is developmentally normal and resolves without intervention
Indicates possible sexual abuse - investigate immediately
rat: Functional encopresis is caused by chronic constipation leading to rectal distension and loss of normal sensation. Treatment: clean-out, maintenance laxatives, fiber/fluids, behavioral retraining.
optRationales: Regression is not the primary explanation — this is a physiological problem requiring medical management, not psychiatric referral first.
Chronic constipation causes rectal distension, loss of normal sensation, and involuntary overflow soiling — the correct explanation for functional encopresis.
This does not resolve without treatment — it requires clean-out, maintenance laxatives, and behavioral retraining.
While abuse can present with encopresis, the most common cause is functional/constipation-related — this should not be the first assumption.

stem: A 10-month-old dropped from the 30th to 2nd percentile. Parent-infant interaction is disengaged. The infant is irritable with poor feeding.
actions: Order full metabolic/genetic workup immediately
Initiate caloric supplementation and model feeding interactions
Reassure family - variation is normal
Refer immediately to CPS
rat: Non-organic FTT: priority is caloric support and modeling positive feeding interactions. Monitor weight trend and feeding interaction quality.

stem: A 3-month-old with unrepaired VSD has poor feeding, diaphoresis with feeding, and RR 68/min. Which nursing interventions are appropriate? Select all that apply.
opts: Feed smaller volumes more frequently
Position flat to promote rest
Administer furosemide as ordered
Monitor daily weights at same time
Limit each feeding to ~30 minutes
Encourage demand feeding
rat: CHF: small frequent feeds reduce cardiac workload; furosemide reduces fluid overload; daily weights track fluid status; time-limit feeds to ~30 min. Semi-Fowler's (not flat) reduces respiratory effort.
optRationales: Smaller, more frequent feeds reduce the cardiac workload required for feeding — appropriate for CHF.
Flat positioning increases cardiac preload and respiratory effort — semi-Fowler's position is preferred.
Furosemide reduces fluid overload and pulmonary congestion — appropriate for CHF management.
Daily weights at the same time detect fluid retention trends early — appropriate monitoring.
Limiting feedings to ~30 minutes prevents excessive energy expenditure from prolonged feeding effort.
Demand feeding can lead to overfeeding and increased cardiac workload — scheduled small feeds are better.

stem: Before giving digoxin to a 4-month-old, the nurse assesses an apical pulse of 96 bpm. Correct action?
opts: Administer - 96 is acceptable
Hold; notify provider - rate below safe threshold for this age
Document and give; bradycardia does not affect dosing
Reassess in 15 minutes
rat: Digoxin is held if apical pulse is less than 90 bpm in infants. Normal infant HR 110-160; 96 bpm represents bradycardia in this context. Hold dose and notify provider immediately.
optRationales: 96 bpm is below the safe threshold of 90 bpm for infants receiving digoxin — administering is incorrect.
Digoxin is held when the apical pulse is below 90 bpm in infants — this is the correct action.
Bradycardia absolutely affects digoxin dosing — it is a direct sign of potential toxicity.
Reassessing in 15 minutes without holding the dose is unsafe — the dose should be held and provider notified immediately.

stem: Safe digoxin range is 8-12 mcg/kg/day divided q12h. Infant weighs 7 kg. Order: digoxin 30 mcg IV q12h. Is this safe or unsafe?
rat: 8-12 mcg/kg/day x 7 kg = 56-84 mcg/day divided by 2 = 28-42 mcg/dose. Order of 30 mcg is within range, so it is SAFE. Always calculate weight-based range before determining safety.

stem: A 6-month-old with TOF becomes acutely cyanotic and hyperpneic during a diaper change. Indicate whether each action is Indicated, Contraindicated, or Non-essential.
rows: Administer IV morphine sulfate as ordered
Calm the infant and reduce stimulation
rat: Knee-chest increases SVR reducing right-to-left shunt. O2 reduces hypoxia. Morphine decreases hyperpnea and anxiety. Calming reduces O2 demand. Defibrillator is non-essential - Tet spells are not arrhythmias.

stem: The parent of a child with TOF asks why the child squats down during play. The nurse's best response is:
opts: Squatting is a sign of muscle weakness from decreased oxygenation
Squatting increases venous return and SVR, reducing the right-to-left shunt
Squatting is a behavioral habit unrelated to the heart defect
Squatting should be discouraged as it increases cardiac workload
rat: Squatting increases SVR and venous return, which reduces the right-to-left shunt through the VSD, improving pulmonary blood flow and oxygenation temporarily.
optRationales: Squatting does not indicate muscle weakness — it is an intentional compensatory maneuver.
Squatting compresses the femoral veins, increasing systemic vascular resistance and venous return, which reduces the right-to-left shunt through the VSD — the correct mechanism.
Squatting in TOF is not a behavioral habit — it is a physiologically driven compensatory maneuver.
Squatting should not be discouraged — it is a beneficial adaptive behavior that improves oxygenation.

stem: A 3-year-old has had fever greater than 39C for 5 days with bilateral non-purulent conjunctival injection, strawberry tongue, erythematous lips, erythema of palms/soles, and cervical lymphadenopathy. Priority treatment?
opts: IV antibiotics and blood cultures
High-dose IVIG and aspirin
Corticosteroids and antipyretics only
Antivirals and cardiac monitoring
rat: Full Kawasaki criteria met. Gold standard: high-dose IVIG (2 g/kg) plus high-dose aspirin to prevent coronary artery aneurysm. Aspirin is specifically indicated here despite usual avoidance in children.
optRationales: IV antibiotics and cultures are appropriate for bacterial infection, not Kawasaki disease.
High-dose IVIG (2 g/kg) plus high-dose aspirin is the gold standard treatment to prevent coronary artery aneurysm — the correct choice.
Corticosteroids are not first-line for Kawasaki disease. Aspirin is specifically indicated here despite the usual avoidance in children.
Antivirals have no role — Kawasaki disease is not caused by a known virus.

stem: A 9-year-old is newly diagnosed with acute rheumatic fever. Which findings are consistent? Select all that apply.
opts: Streptococcal pharyngitis 2-4 weeks prior
Migratory polyarthritis
Painless subcutaneous nodules
Petechial rash on trunk
Sydenham chorea
Elevated ASO titer
rat: Jones Criteria: carditis, migratory polyarthritis, Sydenham chorea, erythema marginatum, subcutaneous nodules. ASO titer confirms prior strep. Petechial rash is NOT part of rheumatic fever.
optRationales: Prior strep pharyngitis (2–4 weeks before) is a known trigger for rheumatic fever — consistent finding.
Migratory polyarthritis is one of the major Jones criteria for rheumatic fever.
Painless subcutaneous nodules are a minor Jones criteria finding.
Petechial rash is not part of rheumatic fever — erythema marginatum is the characteristic rash.
Sydenham chorea (involuntary movements) is a major Jones criteria finding.
Elevated ASO titer confirms prior streptococcal infection — consistent with rheumatic fever.

stem: Nurse auscultates a murmur in a 5-year-old: soft, heard only with firm pressure, no thrill, disappears when standing. Best interpretation?
opts: Grade III; refer for echo
Grade I-II innocent murmur; document and report to provider
Grade IV with thrill; urgent cardiology
Grade II; restrict activities
rat: Barely audible with firm pressure, no thrill, positional variation = Grade I-II innocent murmur. Up to 80% of children have innocent murmurs. No restriction needed.
optRationales: Grade III murmurs are easily heard without stethoscope pressure and may have a thrill — not this presentation.
Barely audible with firm pressure, no thrill, and disappearing when standing are classic Grade I-II innocent murmur characteristics.
Grade IV murmurs have a palpable thrill — not present here.
Grade II murmurs do not require activity restriction — up to 80% of children have innocent murmurs.

stem: Parent notices abdominal swelling while bathing a 3-year-old. Child afebrile, eating well, no pain. Firm, smooth, unilateral mass suspected.
actions: Palpate firmly to characterize the mass
Post 'Do Not Palpate Abdomen' sign
Obtain VS and measure abdominal girth without deep palpation
Prepare child for immediate appendectomy
rat: Smooth firm unilateral toddler mass = Wilms until proven otherwise. Deep palpation can rupture the capsule. Post Do Not Palpate sign. Monitor BP (hypertension via renin) and urine for hematuria.

stem: A 14-month-old has sunken fontanelle, dry mucous membranes, decreased skin turgor, and USG 1.030. HR and BP normal. Degree of dehydration?
opts: Mild (3-5% loss)
Moderate (6-9% loss)
Severe (10% or greater loss)
Not dehydrated
rat: Sunken fontanelle, dry membranes, decreased turgor, USG greater than 1.020 without hemodynamic compromise = moderate dehydration. Severe includes altered mental status, mottling, tachycardia, hypotension.
optRationales: Mild dehydration (3–5% loss) shows only slightly dry mucous membranes and mild thirst — not this clinical picture.
Sunken fontanelle, dry membranes, decreased turgor, and high USG without hemodynamic instability = moderate dehydration (6–9% loss).
Severe dehydration (≥10%) includes altered mental status, mottling, tachycardia, and hypotension — not present here.
The clinical findings clearly indicate dehydration — normal assessment would show no signs.

stem: Parent asks if sports drinks are OK for home rehydration of a 2-year-old with mild gastroenteritis. Best response?
opts: Sports drinks are ideal - replace electrolytes
Apple juice preferred - kids accept it well
Use commercial ORS like Pedialyte; sports drinks have inadequate sodium and excessive sugar
Any clear liquid is fine in adequate volume
rat: WHO/AAP ORS: sodium 45-75 mEq/L, glucose optimized for cotransport. Sports drinks: too little sodium, too much sugar. Apple juice: negligible sodium, high fructose.
optRationales: Sports drinks contain insufficient sodium (20 mEq/L vs 45–75 needed) and excessive sugar — inappropriate for rehydration.
Apple juice has negligible sodium and high fructose — can worsen diarrhea and is not appropriate for rehydration.
Commercial ORS like Pedialyte has the optimal sodium-to-glucose ratio for intestinal cotransport absorption — correct recommendation.
Any clear liquid is insufficient — electrolyte composition matters significantly for effective oral rehydration.

stem: A 2-year-old girl has her second UTI in 6 months. Urine culture grows E. coli; treatment completed. Next anticipated step?
opts: Discharge with fluids; follow-up in 1 year
Renal ultrasound and VCUG referral
Reassure family - very common in girls
12 months prophylactic antibiotics without imaging
rat: Recurrent UTIs in children under 3 years warrant workup for VUR. VCUG is gold standard; renal ultrasound evaluates structural abnormalities.
optRationales: Discharge without imaging is inappropriate for a child with recurrent UTIs under age 3 — workup is required.
Renal ultrasound evaluates structural abnormalities and VCUG is the gold standard for diagnosing vesicoureteral reflux — indicated after second UTI in a child under 3.
Reassurance without workup is inappropriate — recurrent UTIs may indicate underlying anatomical abnormality.
Prophylactic antibiotics without imaging bypasses the diagnostic workup needed to determine if there is an anatomical cause.

stem: Patient A: massive proteinuria, hypoalbuminemia, edema, hyperlipidemia. Patient B: hematuria, hypertension, oliguria, elevated BUN/Cr. Assign each finding.
rat: Nephrotic: frothy urine (proteinuria), periorbital edema. Nephritic: hematuria, hypertension, often post-strep. Periorbital edema appears in both.

stem: Which parent statements indicate correct understanding of tympanostomy tube care? Select all that apply.
opts: My child can swim in any pool without ear protection
I should report purulent ear drainage to the provider
My child may hear differently as pressure equalizes
The tubes are permanent and won't fall out
I should follow provider instructions about water precautions
rat: Report purulent drainage (infection through the tube). Expect temporary hearing change. Tubes extrude in 6-18 months - NOT permanent. Most providers recommend water precautions.
optRationales: Swimming in pools without protection is not recommended — water can enter the middle ear through the tube and cause infection.
Purulent drainage through the tube indicates infection and should be reported to the provider for treatment.
Hearing may change as middle ear pressure equalizes through the tube — this is expected and temporary.
Tympanostomy tubes are not permanent — they typically extrude spontaneously in 6–18 months.
Water precautions (earplugs, avoiding submersion) are typically recommended — the provider will give specific instructions.

stem: Most important instruction for managing atopic dermatitis in a 2-year-old?
opts: Bathe daily in hot water to remove allergens
Apply fragrance-free moisturizer within 3 minutes of bathing while damp
Use fragrance-containing products to soothe
Dress in wool for warmth
rat: Soak and seal: lukewarm bath then fragrance-free emollient within 3 minutes locks in moisture. Hot water, fragrances, and wool worsen barrier disruption and pruritus.
optRationales: Hot water disrupts the skin barrier and worsens pruritus — lukewarm water is recommended.
Applying fragrance-free moisturizer within 3 minutes of bathing while the skin is still damp (soak and seal technique) locks in moisture and repairs the skin barrier — the most important instruction.
Fragrance-containing products are irritants that worsen atopic dermatitis.
Wool is a rough fiber that irritates sensitive skin and worsens eczema — soft cotton clothing is preferred.

stem: Drag each characteristic to the correct infestation. Each zone may receive multiple items.
items: Permethrin 5% cream to entire body overnight
Eggs hatch on human scalp only
rat: Head Lice: nits on hair shaft, spinosad/ivermectin or permethrin 1% rinse, eggs hatch on scalp. Pinworms: nocturnal perianal itch, tape test, mebendazole/albendazole. Scabies: interdigital burrows, permethrin 5% to entire body overnight.

stem: The nurse is teaching a parent about head lice treatment and prevention. Which instructions are correct? Select all that apply.
opts: Machine wash bedding and clothing in hot water
Treat all household members regardless of symptoms
Nits more than 1 cm from the scalp are likely non-viable
Prophylactic treatment of asymptomatic close contacts is always recommended
Retreat in 7-10 days to kill newly hatched nymphs
Shave the child's head to eliminate all nits
rat: Wash bedding/clothing in hot water. Nits greater than 1 cm from scalp are likely non-viable. Retreatment in 7-10 days eliminates newly hatched nymphs. Prophylactic treatment of asymptomatic contacts is NOT routinely recommended. Head shaving is not required.
optRationales: Machine washing bedding and clothing in hot water kills lice and nits on fomites — correct.
Treating all household members prophylactically is NOT routinely recommended — only treat symptomatic individuals and close contacts with confirmed exposure.
Nits found more than 1 cm from the scalp are likely non-viable (empty shells) — correct guidance.
Prophylactic treatment of all asymptomatic close contacts is not standard practice.
Retreatment in 7–10 days kills newly hatched nymphs that may have survived the first treatment — correct.
Head shaving is not necessary or required — nit removal with a fine-tooth comb is sufficient.

stem: A 5-year-old is diagnosed with scabies. How should the parent apply permethrin 5% cream?
opts: Apply only to the scalp and hair; rinse immediately
Apply from neck to toes on entire body, leave on 8-12 hours, then wash off
Apply to affected areas only and rinse immediately
Oral medication only - no topical treatment needed
rat: Permethrin 5% cream is applied to the entire body from the neck down (including under nails) and left on 8-12 hours before washing off.
optRationales: Applying only to the scalp treats head lice, not scabies. Scabies burrows are on the body.
Permethrin 5% cream applied from neck to toes, left on 8–12 hours, then washed off is the correct treatment for scabies.
Applying only to affected areas is insufficient — scabies mites spread throughout the body and must be treated comprehensively.
Topical permethrin is the first-line treatment for scabies — oral ivermectin is an alternative but not the primary recommendation here.

stem: A parent calls about their 6-year-old with intense perianal itching worse at night. Nurse suspects pinworms. Most appropriate diagnostic instruction?
opts: Bring a stool sample in the morning after breakfast
Press clear tape to the perianal area first thing in the morning before bathing or bowel movement
Schedule a colonoscopy
Obtain a blood culture for parasite detection
rat: The tape test (clear tape pressed to perianal area early morning before bathing or defecation) detects Enterobius vermicularis eggs. Stool cultures are rarely positive.
optRationales: A stool sample in the morning after breakfast is unlikely to capture Enterobius eggs, which are deposited perianally at night.
The tape test (pressing clear tape to the perianal area first thing in the morning before bathing or bowel movement) captures Enterobius vermicularis eggs — the standard diagnostic method.
Colonoscopy is not indicated for pinworm diagnosis.
Blood cultures do not detect intestinal parasites.

stem: Developing care plan for a 4-year-old with spastic diplegia CP. Highest-priority intervention for long-term functional outcomes?
opts: Dietary modifications to limit caloric intake
Early referral to PT and OT
Isolation from peers to prevent infection
Prophylactic anticonvulsants for all CP patients
rat: Early intensive PT and OT maximize neuroplasticity and functional independence. Anticonvulsants only if seizures present. Children with CP often need increased calories.
optRationales: Dietary modifications to limit calories are inappropriate — children with CP often have increased caloric needs due to muscle tone.
Early referral to PT and OT maximizes neuroplasticity and functional independence — the highest priority intervention.
Isolation from peers is harmful to development — children with CP benefit from social interaction.
Prophylactic anticonvulsants are not indicated unless the child has seizures — not all CP patients have epilepsy.

stem: Which findings support a diagnosis of Duchenne muscular dystrophy in a 5-year-old boy? Select all that apply.
opts: Gower's sign when rising from floor
Pseudohypertrophy of calf muscles
Markedly elevated creatine kinase (CK)
Intact sensation in all extremities
Strength improves significantly after rest
Symptom onset in early teenage years
rat: DMD: Gower's sign (proximal weakness), calf pseudohypertrophy, markedly elevated CK, preserved sensation (pure motor disease). Symptoms begin age 2-5, not teen years.
optRationales: Gower's sign (using hands to walk up the thighs to rise from the floor) indicates proximal muscle weakness — classic for DMD.
Pseudohypertrophy of the calves results from fat and connective tissue replacing muscle — a hallmark of DMD.
Markedly elevated creatine kinase reflects ongoing muscle fiber destruction — a key diagnostic marker for DMD.
Sensation is preserved in DMD — it is a purely motor disease affecting skeletal muscle.
Strength does not improve significantly after rest in DMD — fatigue is not the primary mechanism.
DMD symptoms begin between ages 2–5, not in teenage years. By the teenage years, most boys are wheelchair-dependent.

stem: A 14-year-old is one day post-posterior spinal fusion for scoliosis. When repositioning, the nurse should:
opts: Rotate hips first, then shoulders
Use log-rolling technique to maintain spinal alignment
Have patient pull on side rail to turn
Allow patient to bend forward to edge of bed
rat: Log-rolling moves head, shoulders, and hips as one unit. Rotating segments independently risks surgical repair damage and neurological injury.
optRationales: Rotating hips first then shoulders violates spinal alignment — this technique risks injury to the surgical repair.
Log-rolling (moving head, shoulders, and hips as one unit) maintains spinal alignment and protects the surgical repair.
Using the side rail to pull could cause twisting of the spine — contraindicated post-spinal fusion.
Bending forward flexes the spine — absolutely contraindicated after posterior spinal fusion.

stem: A 6-year-old boy has a 2-month history of painless limp. Afebrile. X-ray: flattening and fragmentation of left femoral head.
actions: Prepare for emergent surgical hip drainage
Initiate weight-bearing restrictions per orthopedic orders
Apply bilateral skeletal traction immediately
Provide pain management and arrange orthopedic referral
monitor: Pain level and gait with weight-bearing
Range of motion of affected hip
Serum WBC and CRP for infection
rat: Painless limp plus afebrile plus X-ray changes = LCP disease. Management: non-weight-bearing, containment, orthopedic referral. Monitor pain/gait and hip ROM.

stem: Triage four pediatric patients. Match each finding with the correct interpretation.
rows: Substernal and intercostal retractions in a 4-year-old
rat: Newborn RR 30-60 = normal. HR 160 crying 6-month-old = upper limit of normal. BP 90/60 in 10-year-old = normal. Retractions in any child = increased work of breathing = immediate intervention.

stem: A 10-year-old with leukemia asks what her new medication does. Parents asked staff not to tell her anything. Most ethically appropriate action?
opts: Respect parents' wishes and withhold information
Explain that children 7+ have capacity to assent; advocate for age-appropriate disclosure
Refer conflict to chaplain only
Tell child all clinical details without parental consent
rat: School-age children (7+) have developmental capacity to understand and participate in care decisions. Nurse advocates for the child's right to assent, facilitates communication, and involves child life specialists.
optRationales: Respecting the parents' wishes above the child's right to information violates the child's developing autonomy and ethical principles of beneficence.
Children aged 7 and older have the developmental capacity to understand and participate in care — the nurse should advocate for age-appropriate disclosure and involve child life specialists.
Referring only to the chaplain does not address the ethical issue — it needs active nursing advocacy.
Sharing all clinical details without parental involvement may harm the relationship and family dynamics — collaboration is key.

stem: Which statement most accurately distinguishes pediatric assent from consent?
opts: Assent and consent are legally identical for children over age 12
Assent is the child's affirmative agreement to participate; consent is legal authorization from a parent/guardian
Assent is only required for children 16 and older
Parental consent is not required once a child provides assent
rat: Assent = the child's meaningful agreement to care (ethically important, not legally binding). Consent = legal authorization from parent/guardian. Both are ideally obtained.
optRationales: Assent and consent are not legally identical — consent has legal authority; assent is ethically important but not legally binding.
Assent is the child's meaningful agreement to participate in care (ethically important, not legally binding). Consent is legal authorization from a parent or guardian — both are ideally obtained.
Assent is relevant from approximately age 7 onward when children develop cognitive capacity — not only at age 16.
Parental consent is still required even when a child provides assent, except in specific circumstances (emancipated minors, etc.).

stem: Which findings would suggest an organic rather than non-organic etiology for FTT? Select all that apply.
opts: Warm, engaged, and responsive parent-infant interaction
Symptoms present from birth including poor latch
Adequate formula availability in the home
Abnormal newborn screening results
Vomiting and diarrhea present from early infancy
Normal developmental milestones
rat: Organic FTT clues: symptoms from birth (suggests metabolic/structural cause), abnormal newborn screen (inborn error of metabolism), and persistent vomiting/diarrhea from early infancy. Engaged parenting and adequate food access suggest non-organic.
optRationales: Warm, engaged parenting and adequate food availability suggest non-organic (psychosocial) FTT, not organic.
Symptoms present from birth including poor latch suggest a structural or metabolic cause — consistent with organic FTT.
Adequate formula availability suggests the problem is not nutritional access — points away from organic.
Abnormal newborn screening may indicate an inborn error of metabolism — a cause of organic FTT.
Persistent vomiting and diarrhea from early infancy suggest a metabolic or structural GI cause — organic FTT.
Normal developmental milestones suggest the underlying condition has not caused significant systemic impact — less consistent with severe organic FTT.

stem: The nurse is teaching a parent how to recognize worsening CHF in their infant at home. Which sign should prompt an immediate call?
opts: A single missed feeding
Weight gain of more than 50 grams (~2 oz) per day
Sleeping 3-4 hours between feedings
One episode of spitting up after a feeding
rat: Rapid weight gain (greater than 50 g/day in infants) indicates fluid retention and worsening CHF. Normal sleep, missed feedings, and occasional spitting up are normal infant variations.
optRationales: A single missed feeding is common in infants and does not indicate worsening CHF.
Weight gain greater than 50 g/day in infants indicates fluid retention and worsening CHF — this should prompt immediate contact with the provider.
Sleeping 3–4 hours between feedings is normal infant behavior.
Occasional spitting up is normal and does not indicate worsening cardiac status.

stem: A child with Wilms tumor is being prepared for surgery. The nurse observes a family member performing abdominal massage. Priority action?
opts: Allow the massage - it promotes comfort
Educate the family member to stop immediately and explain the reason
Document the finding and report at the next shift change
Ask the provider if massage is acceptable
rat: Abdominal palpation or massage in Wilms tumor risks rupturing the tumor capsule and seeding malignant cells. Stop immediately and educate the family clearly.
optRationales: Allowing abdominal massage risks rupturing the Wilms tumor capsule and seeding malignant cells — it must be stopped.
Stopping the massage immediately and educating the family is the priority action — prevents tumor capsule rupture.
Documenting and reporting at shift change is too delayed — this requires immediate action.
Asking the provider is too slow — the nurse must act immediately to stop the massage.

stem: A 4-year-old treated for Kawasaki disease is being discharged. Most important long-term follow-up teaching?
opts: No follow-up needed once fever resolves
Echocardiograms are needed to monitor for coronary artery aneurysms
Avoid all physical activity for 1 year
Aspirin therapy must continue for exactly 2 weeks only
rat: Coronary artery aneurysm is the most serious complication. Serial echocardiograms (at diagnosis, 2 weeks, 6-8 weeks) are essential. Aspirin duration depends on echo findings.
optRationales: Follow-up is critical — coronary artery aneurysm can develop even after fever resolves.
Serial echocardiograms are essential to monitor for coronary artery aneurysm, the most serious complication of Kawasaki disease.
Physical activity restriction for a full year is excessive — activity limitations depend on coronary involvement.
Aspirin duration depends on echocardiographic findings — it may continue well beyond 2 weeks if aneurysms are present.

stem: A child is diagnosed with acute rheumatic fever with carditis. Anticipated long-term management?
opts: No further treatment once inflammation resolves
Monthly benzathine penicillin G (or daily oral penicillin) for secondary prophylaxis
Annual influenza vaccination only
Avoid all physical activity permanently
rat: Long-term antibiotic prophylaxis prevents recurrent strep infections that could worsen valve damage. Duration: 10 years or until age 21 (longer with significant carditis).
optRationales: Inflammation resolving does not eliminate the risk of recurrent strep — prophylaxis is ongoing.
Monthly benzathine penicillin G or daily oral penicillin provides secondary prophylaxis against strep, preventing recurrent rheumatic fever and progressive valve damage — correct.
Annual influenza vaccine is recommended for many patients but does not substitute for strep prophylaxis.
Permanent activity restriction is not appropriate — restrictions depend on degree of cardiac involvement.

stem: The nurse is planning care for a child with cerebral palsy. Which considerations are important? Select all that apply.
opts: CP is a static (non-progressive) brain lesion
All children with CP have intellectual disability
Caloric needs may be higher due to increased muscle tone
Drooling and dysphagia may be present depending on type
Seizure disorder may coexist with CP
Hip subluxation and scoliosis are potential complications
rat: CP is a static lesion. NOT all children with CP have intellectual disability. Increased muscle tone raises caloric needs. Oromotor involvement causes drooling/dysphagia. Seizures occur in ~30-50%. Hip subluxation and scoliosis are common orthopedic complications.
optRationales: CP is caused by a non-progressive brain lesion — the injury does not worsen over time, though functional abilities may change with growth.
Not all children with CP have intellectual disability — CP affects motor function primarily; cognitive involvement varies widely.
Increased muscle tone (spasticity) raises metabolic demands — many children with CP need increased caloric intake.
Oromotor involvement in some types of CP causes drooling and dysphagia.
Seizures occur in approximately 30–50% of children with CP.
Hip subluxation and scoliosis are common orthopedic complications due to muscle imbalance.

stem: The nurse is assessing a 6-week-old infant for dehydration. Which finding is unique to infants compared to older children?
opts: Dry mucous membranes
Decreased skin turgor
Sunken anterior fontanelle
Decreased urine output
rat: A sunken anterior fontanelle is unique to infants (closes 12-18 months). Dry mucous membranes, decreased turgor, and decreased urine output are signs across all ages.
optRationales: Dry mucous membranes are a sign of dehydration across all ages — not unique to infants.
Decreased skin turgor is seen across all ages with dehydration — not specific to infants.
The anterior fontanelle closes at 12–18 months — sunken fontanelle is unique to infants as a dehydration sign.
Decreased urine output is a sign of dehydration at all ages — not specific to infants.

stem: The nurse receives handoff on four patients. Which should be assessed first? Select all that apply.
opts: 3-year-old with substernal retractions and HR 148
7-year-old with mild expiratory wheeze, SpO2 97%, RR 24
5-year-old with nasal flaring, grunting, and SpO2 90%
10-year-old with clear breath sounds and RR 20
2-year-old with intercostal retractions and inability to feed
rat: Priority patients: substernal retractions plus tachycardia = increased WOB; SpO2 90% plus grunting plus nasal flaring = severe respiratory distress; inability to feed with retractions = significant respiratory compromise.
optRationales: Substernal retractions plus tachycardia indicate significant increased WOB — this child should be assessed first.
SpO2 97% with mild wheeze and slightly elevated RR is a lower-priority finding — stable.
SpO2 90% with grunting and nasal flaring indicates severe respiratory distress — assess immediately.
Clear breath sounds and normal RR indicate a stable patient — lowest priority.
Intercostal retractions with inability to feed indicate significant respiratory compromise affecting feeding — assess promptly.

stem: The nurse notes digital clubbing in a 7-year-old. Which statements about clubbing are accurate? Select all that apply.
opts: Clubbing results from chronic tissue hypoxia and altered peripheral blood flow
Clubbing reverses completely within hours of restoring normal oxygenation
The Schamroth window test can be used to assess for clubbing
Clubbing is a normal variant in children requiring no evaluation
Clubbing may be seen in cystic fibrosis and cyanotic congenital heart defects
rat: Clubbing reflects chronic hypoxia. The Schamroth test (diamond window lost when dorsal surfaces apposed) is a screening method. NOT a normal variant. Reversal takes months, not hours. Seen in CF, cyanotic CHD, chronic lung disease.
optRationales: Clubbing results from chronic tissue hypoxia causing periosteal proliferation and altered peripheral blood flow — correct.
Reversal of clubbing takes months, not hours — it is a gradual structural change.
The Schamroth window test (loss of diamond window when dorsal surfaces of fingers are apposed) is used to screen for clubbing.
Clubbing is never a normal variant — it always indicates underlying pathology.
Clubbing is seen in CF, cyanotic congenital heart defects, and chronic pulmonary disease — correct.

stem: The nurse admits a patient to the critical care unit following a motorcycle crash. Assessment findings include BP 100/50 mmHg, HR 58 beats/min, RR 30 breaths/min, and temperature 100.5°F. The patient is lethargic. Which nursing action is most important to include in this patient's plan of care?
opts: Frequent neurological assessments
Side-to-side position changes
Range of motion to extremities
Frequent oropharyngeal suctioning
rat: The newly admitted patient has an altered neurological status, making frequent neurological assessments the priority. Position changes, ROM, and oral suctioning may be part of care but should not be routinely performed in the setting of increased ICP unless specifically indicated.
optRationales: Frequent neurological assessments are the priority for a patient with altered mental status and signs of increased ICP — correct.
Side-to-side position changes can increase ICP — they should be done carefully if at all, not routinely.
Range of motion is appropriate long-term but is not the priority in an acutely unstable patient with likely ICP elevation.
Oropharyngeal suctioning stimulates coughing and Valsalva, which increases ICP — not appropriate routinely.

stem: A patient with a head injury has an ICP of 18 mmHg, BP 144/90 mmHg, and MAP 108 mmHg. Using the formula CPP = MAP - ICP, calculate the cerebral perfusion pressure. Enter your answer as a number.
rat: CPP = MAP - ICP = 108 - 18 = 90 mmHg. This falls within the normal acceptable range (70-100 mmHg).

stem: While caring for a patient with a traumatic brain injury, the nurse assesses an ICP of 20 mmHg and a CPP of 85 mmHg. What is the best interpretation?
opts: Both pressures are high
Both pressures are low
ICP is high; CPP is normal
ICP is high; CPP is low
rat: Normal ICP is less than 15 mmHg, so 20 mmHg is elevated. Normal CPP is 70-100 mmHg, so 85 mmHg is within normal range.
optRationales: Both pressures being high is incorrect — CPP of 85 mmHg is within the normal range of 70–100 mmHg.
Both pressures being low is incorrect — ICP of 20 mmHg is above the normal threshold of less than 15 mmHg.
ICP of 20 mmHg is elevated (normal <15). CPP of 85 mmHg is within the normal range of 70–100 mmHg — correct interpretation.
CPP of 85 mmHg is not low — it is within the acceptable range.

stem: The nurse is caring for a mechanically ventilated patient with a sustained ICP of 18 mmHg who needs multiple nursing interventions. What is the best action?
opts: Hyperoxygenate during endotracheal suctioning
Elevate the patient's head of bed 30 degrees
Apply bilateral heel protectors after repositioning
Provide rest periods between nursing interventions
rat: Sustained ICP increases lasting longer than 5 minutes should be avoided. Spacing nursing care activities to allow rest between them is the priority intervention.
optRationales: Hyperoxygenation before suctioning is appropriate technique but is not the overarching intervention for sustained ICP elevation.
HOB elevation to 30 degrees promotes venous drainage and reduces ICP — appropriate but not the most important when sustained increases occur.
Heel protectors and repositioning address skin integrity — important but not the ICP priority here.
Spacing nursing activities with rest periods between them prevents sustained ICP increases lasting longer than 5 minutes — the priority intervention for managing cumulative stimulation.

stem: While caring for a patient with a basilar skull fracture, the nurse assesses clear drainage from the patient's left naris. What is the best nursing action?
opts: Have the patient blow the nose until clear
Insert bilateral cotton nasal packing
Place a nasal drip pad under the nose
Suction the left nares until the drainage clears
rat: In suspected CSF leak, drainage should be unobstructed and free-flowing. A nasal drip pad allows collection and assessment. Patients should NOT blow their nose as this aggravates the dural tear. Suctioning through the nose risks penetrating the brain through the dural tear.
optRationales: Blowing the nose is absolutely contraindicated — it aggravates the dural tear and risks introducing infection intracranially.
Bilateral nasal packing could obstruct drainage and increase ICP — contraindicated.
A nasal drip pad allows CSF to flow freely, permits assessment of amount and character, and avoids any obstruction — correct.
Suctioning through the nares risks penetrating the brain through the dural defect — absolutely contraindicated.

stem: A patient struck on the head was briefly unconscious at the scene, is now conscious in the ED with a GCS of 15, but 1 hour later has a GCS of 3. What is the priority nursing action?
opts: Stimulate the patient hourly
Continue to monitor the patient
Elevate the head of the bed
Notify the primary care provider immediately
rat: Brief unconsciousness followed by a lucid interval and then rapid deterioration is the classic presentation of an epidural or acute subdural hematoma — a neurological emergency. The provider must be notified immediately.
optRationales: Stimulating the patient does not address the neurological emergency — this is a rapid deterioration requiring immediate provider notification.
Continuing to monitor is inappropriate — the GCS dropped from 15 to 3, indicating a neurological emergency.
Elevating the HOB may be part of management but is not the priority over notifying the provider.

A brief lucid interval followed by rapid neurological deterioration is the classic presentation of epidural or acute subdural hematoma — the provider must be notified immediately for emergent intervention.

stem: A patient with an ICP of 18 mmHg and GCS of 3 receives mannitol. Which assessment finding requires further action?
opts: ICP of 10 mmHg
CPP of 70 mmHg
GCS score of 5
CVP of 2 mmHg
rat: ICP 10 mmHg and CPP 70 mmHg are within normal limits. GCS 5 shows slight improvement. A CVP of 2 mmHg indicates hypovolemia, which threatens cerebral perfusion and requires further action.
optRationales: ICP of 10 mmHg is within normal limits (<15 mmHg) — no action needed.
CPP of 70 mmHg is at the lower end of acceptable range (70–100 mmHg) — no immediate action needed.
GCS improvement from 3 to 5 shows slight positive neurological change — not a concern.
CVP of 2 mmHg indicates hypovolemia — inadequate intravascular volume threatens cerebral perfusion pressure and requires action.

stem: A mechanically ventilated patient with a brain injury has a PaCO2 of 60 mmHg. This value will have which effect on cerebral blood flow?
opts: Altered CSF production and reabsorption
Decreased cerebral blood volume due to vessel constriction
Increased cerebral blood volume due to vessel dilation
No effect — PaCO2 of 60 mmHg is normal
rat: PaCO2 of 60 mmHg is elevated (normal 35-45 mmHg). Elevated CO2 causes cerebral vasodilation, increasing cerebral blood volume and ICP.
optRationales: Altered CSF production and reabsorption is not the primary mechanism of CO2-mediated cerebral blood flow changes.
Elevated CO2 causes vasodilation, not constriction — this is the opposite effect.
Elevated PaCO2 (normal 35–45) causes cerebral vasodilation, increasing cerebral blood volume and ICP — correct.
PaCO2 of 60 mmHg is significantly elevated — it is not normal and will have a major effect on cerebral blood flow.

stem: A patient with a skull fracture has a GCS of 3, BP 100/70 mmHg, HR 55 beats/min, RR 10 breaths/min, and SpO2 94% on 3L O2 via nasal cannula. What is the priority nursing action?
opts: Monitor the patient's airway patency
Elevate the head of the patient's bed
Increase supplemental oxygen delivery
Support bony prominences with padding
rat: GCS of 3 indicates deep coma. With a RR of only 10 breaths/min, the nurse must prioritize airway patency. A slow RR may cause CO2 retention, further increasing ICP via cerebral vasodilation.
optRationales: Airway patency is the priority — GCS of 3 with RR of only 10 risks CO2 retention, vasodilation, and further ICP increase.
HOB elevation is appropriate but secondary to airway management.
Increasing supplemental oxygen addresses hypoxia but does not correct the hypoventilation causing CO2 retention.
Supporting bony prominences addresses skin integrity — important but not the immediate priority.

stem: A patient admitted after a fall from a 10-foot ladder is awake, alert, with bruising behind the left ear and straw-colored drainage from the left nare. What is the most appropriate nursing action?
opts: Insert bilateral ear plugs
Monitor airway patency
Maintain neutral head position
Apply a small nasal drip pad
rat: Straw-colored nasal drainage may indicate a CSF leak from a basilar skull fracture. A nasal drip pad allows drainage to flow freely and permits assessment of amount and characteristics.
optRationales: Ear plugs could prevent assessment of ongoing drainage and are not appropriate management.
Monitoring airway patency is always important but is not the most specific action for this presentation.
Neutral head position prevents venous obstruction but does not address the drainage directly.
A nasal drip pad allows straw-colored drainage (possible CSF) to flow freely and be assessed — the most appropriate action.

stem: A patient with a closed head injury is alert with BP 130/90 mmHg, HR 60 beats/min, RR 18 breaths/min, and temperature 102°F. What are the priority nursing actions to reduce the risk of increased ICP?
opts: Ensure adequate periods of rest between nursing interventions
Insert an oral airway and monitor respiratory rate and depth
Maintain neutral head alignment and avoid extreme hip flexion
Reduce ambient room temperature and administer antipyretics
rat: Fever increases metabolic demands, which increases cerebral blood flow and ICP. Cooling measures and antipyretics are the priority. Inserting an oral airway in an alert patient is contraindicated.
optRationales: Ensuring rest periods is appropriate for sustained ICP elevation but is not the priority here with a fever of 102°F.
Inserting an oral airway in an alert patient is contraindicated — it could trigger gagging, vomiting, and increased ICP.
Neutral head alignment is appropriate but secondary to treating the fever in this scenario.
Fever increases cerebral metabolic demand and blood flow, worsening ICP — antipyretics and cooling measures are the priority intervention.

stem: The nurse finds a patient in the neuro ICU sitting in a chair experiencing a tonic-clonic seizure. What is the best nursing action?
opts: Assist the patient to the floor and provide soft head support
Insert a nasogastric tube and connect to continuous wall suction
Open the patient's mouth and insert a padded tongue blade
Restrain the patient's extremities until the seizure subsides
rat: The patient should be assisted to the floor with the head adequately supported. Forceful tongue blade insertion is contraindicated — jaws are clenched. Restraining a patient during a seizure can cause trauma and is not standard of care.
optRationales: Assisting the patient to the floor protects against head injury and allows the seizure to proceed safely — correct first action.
Inserting a nasogastric tube during an active seizure is dangerous and not indicated.
Forcibly inserting a tongue blade during tonic-clonic activity risks injury to the patient and the nurse — contraindicated.
Restraining a seizing patient can cause fractures and other injuries — never restrain during a seizure.

stem: A mechanically ventilated patient with TBI needs optimal gas exchange. Which ABG value indicates this?
opts: pH 7.38; PaCO2 55 mmHg; HCO3 22 mEq/L; PaO2 85 mmHg
pH 7.38; PaCO2 40 mmHg; HCO3 24 mEq/L; PaO2 70 mmHg
pH 7.38; PaCO2 35 mmHg; HCO3 24 mEq/L; PaO2 85 mmHg
pH 7.38; PaCO2 28 mmHg; HCO3 26 mEq/L; PaO2 65 mmHg
rat: Optimal gas exchange in TBI requires PaCO2 35-45 mmHg (prevents vasodilation or ischemia) and PaO2 above 80 mmHg for adequate cerebral oxygenation. Option C is the only one meeting both criteria.
optRationales: PaCO2 of 55 is elevated — this causes vasodilation and increases ICP. PaO2 of 85 is acceptable but this option fails due to elevated CO2.
PaO2 of 70 is borderline low — inadequate cerebral oxygenation. Despite normal CO2, this is not optimal.
PaCO2 of 35 is within normal range (prevents vasodilation), and PaO2 of 85 provides adequate cerebral oxygenation — optimal ABG for TBI management.
PaCO2 of 28 is below normal — this causes vasoconstriction and ischemia. PaO2 of 65 is also inadequate.

stem: A patient with a complete cervical spine injury reports a severe headache. BP 180/90 mmHg, HR 60 beats/min, RR 24 breaths/min, and 50 mL urine output in the past 4 hours. What is the best action?
opts: Administer acetaminophen as ordered for the headache
Assess for a kinked urinary catheter and assess for bowel impaction
Encourage the patient to take slow, deep breaths
Notify the physician of the patient's blood pressure
rat: This presentation is classic autonomic dysreflexia. A kinked catheter causing bladder distention or fecal impaction are the most common triggers. Identifying and removing the cause is the first priority.
optRationales: Treating the headache with acetaminophen does not address the life-threatening cause — autonomic dysreflexia requires identifying and removing the trigger.
Autonomic dysreflexia is most commonly triggered by bladder distension (kinked catheter) or bowel impaction — identifying and removing the cause is the first priority.
Slow deep breaths do not address the autonomic crisis — the trigger must be removed.
Notifying the physician is important but secondary to first identifying and removing the trigger — bladder and bowel assessment must happen immediately.

stem: The nurse admits a patient to the ED with new onset of slurred speech and right-sided weakness. What is the priority nursing action to ensure effective care?
opts: Assess for the presence of a headache
Assess the patient's general orientation
Determine the patient's drug allergies
Determine the time of symptom onset
rat: Thrombolytics for ischemic stroke must be given within 3 hours of symptom onset. Determining the exact time of onset is the critical first step — it determines eligibility for tPA.
optRationales: Headache assessment is important but secondary — knowing the time of symptom onset determines tPA eligibility.
Orientation assessment is part of the neurological exam but is secondary to determining tPA eligibility window.
Drug allergies are important but not the most critical first step.
Time of symptom onset is the most critical piece of information — tPA must be given within 3–4.5 hours of onset, making this the absolute first priority.

stem: Which patient in the emergency department should the charge nurse evaluate first?
opts: A patient with a complete spinal injury at the C5 dermatome level
A patient with a GCS of 15 on 3-L nasal cannula
An alert patient with a subdural bleed complaining of a headache
An ischemic stroke patient with a BP of 190/100 mmHg
rat: A C5 complete spinal injury is at risk for ineffective breathing patterns due to phrenic nerve involvement and should be assessed immediately for airway compromise.
optRationales: A C5 complete spinal injury places the phrenic nerve (C3–C5) at risk — respiratory compromise can occur suddenly and requires immediate assessment.
GCS of 15 on 3L NC indicates a stable, conscious patient — lower priority.
An alert patient with subdural bleed and headache is concerning but currently stable — lower priority than the airway risk.
BP of 190/100 in ischemic stroke requires management but the patient is not in immediate airway danger.

stem: The nurse admits a patient to the ED with a suspected cervical spine injury. What is the priority nursing action?
opts: Keep the neck in the hyperextended position
Maintain proper head and neck alignment
Prepare for immediate endotracheal intubation
Remove cervical collar upon arrival to the ED
rat: Proper head and neck alignment may prevent further spinal cord damage. Hyperextension is contraindicated. The cervical collar should remain in place until injury has been ruled out.
optRationales: Hyperextension of a suspected cervical spine injury risks additional cord damage — contraindicated.
Maintaining proper head and neck alignment prevents additional spinal cord injury — the priority action.
Immediate intubation may ultimately be needed but is not the first action — airway assessment and alignment come first.
Removing the cervical collar before injury is ruled out risks cord injury — the collar must remain in place.

stem: A patient is 3 days post complete cervical spine injury at the C3 level and is in spinal shock following emergent intubation and mechanical ventilation. What is the priority nursing action?
opts: Maintain body temperature
Monitor blood pressure
Pad all bony prominences
Use proper hand washing
rat: Maintaining spinal cord perfusion is critical. Blood pressure monitoring is the priority because hypotension during spinal shock can worsen ischemic injury to the cord.
optRationales: Body temperature maintenance is important (spinal shock impairs thermoregulation) but is secondary to perfusion.
Blood pressure monitoring is the priority — hypotension during spinal shock can worsen ischemic injury to the cord by reducing spinal cord perfusion.
Padding bony prominences prevents pressure injury — important but not the immediate priority.
Hand washing prevents infection — always important but not the immediate clinical priority here.

stem: A provider orders glucocorticoids for a 70-kg patient with a complete spinal cord injury: 30 mg/kg IV over 15 minutes, followed 45 minutes later by 5.4 mg/kg/hr for 23 hours. Calculate the total 24-hour dose in mg. Enter the number only.
rat: Bolus: 30 mg/kg x 70 kg = 2,100 mg. Infusion: 5.4 mg/kg/hr x 70 kg x 23 hours = 8,694 mg. Total: 2,100 + 8,694 = 10,794 mg.

stem: A patient received from the ED after a closed head injury has a ventriculostomy placed. Vitals: BP 100/60 mmHg, HR 52 beats/min, RR 24 breaths/min, SpO2 97%, GCS 4, ICP 18 mmHg. Which ordered intervention should the nurse implement first?
opts: Mannitol 1 g intravenous
Portable chest X-ray
Seizure precautions
Ancef 1 g intravenous
rat: With a GCS of 4 and ICP of 18 mmHg, mannitol is the priority intervention to reduce intracranial pressure. A portable CXR and seizure precautions are appropriate but not the immediate priority.
optRationales: Mannitol is the priority to reduce elevated ICP (18 mmHg) with a GCS of 4 — the most urgent intervention.
Portable CXR is important post-intubation but is secondary to managing the acute ICP elevation.
Seizure precautions are appropriate but secondary to reducing ICP.
Ancef (prophylactic antibiotic) is important for ventriculostomy but secondary to the immediate ICP management.

stem: A patient 5 days post-clipping of an anterior communicating artery aneurysm for subarachnoid hemorrhage is more lethargic than the previous hour. BP 95/50 mmHg, HR 110 beats/min, SpO2 95%, temperature 101.5°F. Which order should the nurse implement first?
opts: Blood cultures x2 for temperature greater than 101°F
Acetaminophen 650 mg per rectum
500 mL albumin infusion intravenously
Decadron 20 mg IV push every 4 hours
rat: Cerebral vasospasm is a life-threatening complication following subarachnoid hemorrhage. Volume expansion with 500 mL albumin is the priority for BP 95/50 mmHg to prevent vasospasm and ensure cerebral perfusion.
optRationales: Blood cultures are appropriate for fever but secondary to addressing the hemodynamic compromise threatening cerebral perfusion.
Acetaminophen addresses fever but does not treat the hypovolemia causing hemodynamic compromise.
Volume expansion with 500 mL albumin directly addresses the hypotension (BP 95/50) and low CVP — the priority to prevent vasospasm and ensure cerebral perfusion post-SAH.
Decadron may have a role in reducing cerebral edema but is secondary to volume expansion in this scenario.

stem: A nurse begins a nicardipine infusion at 5 mg/hr for a patient following a subarachnoid hemorrhage with baseline BP 170/100 mmHg. Five minutes later, BP is 160/90 mmHg. What is the best action?
opts: Stop the infusion for 5 minutes
Increase the dose by 2.5 mg/hr
Notify the physician of the BP
Begin weaning the infusion
rat: BP remains dangerously elevated. Increasing the dose by 2.5 mg/hr is appropriate to achieve BP control before the aneurysm is secured. Stopping or weaning the infusion before the target BP is reached is contraindicated.
optRationales: Stopping the infusion removes the treatment — BP remains dangerously elevated and the aneurysm is not yet secured.
Increasing by 2.5 mg/hr is appropriate — BP remains elevated and the target has not been achieved. Dose escalation is needed.
Notifying the physician is not necessary yet — BP has improved slightly and standard titration protocol should continue.
Weaning is contraindicated — the aneurysm is not yet secured and BP is still dangerously elevated.

stem: The nurse is preparing to administer a routine dose of phenytoin. The provider orders phenytoin 500 mg IV every 6 hours. What is the best action?
opts: Administer over 2 minutes
Administer with 0.9% normal saline IV
Contact the primary care provider to discuss the order
Assess cardiac rhythm
rat: The ordered dose is an inappropriate maintenance dose — typical phenytoin maintenance dosing is 100 mg IV every 8 hours, not 500 mg every 6 hours. The nurse should contact the physician to clarify.
optRationales: IV push administration would cause cardiac toxicity — phenytoin must always be infused slowly.
Phenytoin should be administered with normal saline — it precipitates in dextrose solutions. However, the dose is the primary concern here.
The ordered dose of 500 mg q6h is far above the typical maintenance dose of 100 mg q8h — the nurse must contact the provider before administering.
Cardiac monitoring during phenytoin administration is appropriate, but addressing the unsafe dose order takes priority.

stem: A patient is admitted to the ED in status epilepticus with BP 160/100 mmHg, HR 145 beats/min, SpO2 96% on 100% O2. After establishing IV access, which order should the nurse implement first?
opts: Obtain stat serum electrolytes
Administer lorazepam
Obtain stat portable chest X-ray
Administer phenytoin
rat: Lorazepam is the first-line medication for status epilepticus and should be administered immediately. Phenytoin is used only when lorazepam fails or intermittent seizures persist beyond 20 minutes.
optRationales: Serum electrolytes are important but secondary to stopping the seizure with medication.
Lorazepam is the first-line medication for status epilepticus — it should be administered immediately after IV access is established.
Portable CXR is secondary to stopping the seizure.
Phenytoin is used only when benzodiazepines fail or for intermittent seizures persisting beyond 20 minutes — it is not first-line.

stem: The provider orders fosphenytoin 1.5 g IV loading dose for a 75-kg patient in status epilepticus. What is the most important action by the nurse?
opts: Contact the primary care provider to discuss the order
Administer the drug at a slow infusion rate
Mix medication with 0.9% normal saline
Administer via central line
rat: The ordered dose is appropriate for a 75-kg patient. The most important action is to administer at a slow infusion rate. Fosphenytoin does not need to be mixed with normal saline or administered via central line.
optRationales: The dose is appropriate for a 75 kg patient (standard is 15–20 mg PE/kg) — no need to contact the provider.
Slow infusion rate is the most important action for fosphenytoin — rapid infusion can cause cardiovascular toxicity.
Fosphenytoin can be administered with normal saline or dextrose — it does not need to be mixed specifically.
Fosphenytoin does not require central line access — unlike phenytoin, it can be given peripherally.

stem: The nurse is to administer 100 mg phenytoin IV. Vitals include BP 90/60 mmHg, HR 52 beats/min. To prevent complications, what is the best action?
opts: Administer over 2 minutes
Administer over 5 minutes
Mix medication with 0.9% normal saline
Administer via central line
rat: In the presence of hypotension and bradycardia, phenytoin must be infused slowly. Administering over 2 minutes is too fast and may worsen hemodynamic compromise. A slower rate over 5 minutes is safer.
optRationales: Administering over 2 minutes is too fast given the patient's hypotension and bradycardia — cardiovascular toxicity risk.
Administering over 5 minutes at a slower rate is safer given the hemodynamic instability — correct.
Phenytoin precipitates in dextrose but must be mixed with normal saline — not the most important issue given the hemodynamic instability.
Central line is not required for phenytoin — peripheral administration is acceptable.

stem: A patient admitted with subarachnoid hemorrhage following surgical repair has BP 90/60 mmHg, HR 115 beats/min, GCS 4, and CVP 2 mmHg. Which order is highest priority?
opts: Furosemide 20 mg IV push as needed
500 mL albumin IV infusion
Decadron 10 mg IV push
Dilantin 50 mg IV push
rat: CVP 2 mmHg, BP 90/60 mmHg, and HR 115 indicate hypovolemia threatening cerebral perfusion. Volume expansion with 500 mL albumin is the priority. Furosemide is contraindicated in a low-volume state.
optRationales: Furosemide in the setting of hypovolemia (CVP 2, BP 90/60) would worsen the hemodynamic compromise and threaten cerebral perfusion.
Volume expansion with 500 mL albumin addresses the hypovolemia (CVP 2) and hypotension, preventing cerebral vasospasm post-SAH — highest priority.
Decadron may be appropriate but secondary to volume resuscitation.
Dilantin may be appropriate for seizure prophylaxis but secondary to the hemodynamic emergency.

stem: After receiving hand-off report, which patient should the evening charge nurse assess first?
opts: A patient with meningitis complaining of photophobia
A mechanically ventilated patient with a GCS of 6
A patient with bacterial meningitis on droplet precautions
A patient with an ICP of 20 mmHg and an oral temperature of 104°F
rat: An elevated ICP of 20 mmHg combined with a temperature of 104°F is the most concerning combination — fever increases metabolic demands and cerebral blood flow, compounding the elevated ICP.
optRationales: Photophobia with meningitis is uncomfortable but not the most urgent finding.
A mechanically ventilated patient with GCS 6 is critically ill but the GCS is known from handoff.
Bacterial meningitis on droplet precautions is important but the patient is already on precautions.

ICP of 20 mmHg combined with a temperature of 104°F is the most dangerous combination — fever dramatically increases cerebral metabolic demands and blood flow, compounding the elevated ICP. This patient needs immediate attention.

stem: The nurse just received a patient from the ED with a diagnosis of bacterial meningitis. To prevent nosocomial spread, what is the best action?
opts: Implement droplet precautions upon admission
Wash hands thoroughly before leaving the room
Scrub the hub of all central line ports prior to use
Dispose of all bloody dressings in biohazard bags
rat: Droplet precautions are required for bacterial meningitis until 24 hours after antibiotic initiation. Hand hygiene and hub scrubbing reduce infection risk but are not sufficient alone for bacterial meningitis.
optRationales: Droplet precautions must be implemented upon admission for bacterial meningitis — before any other care — to prevent nosocomial transmission. Precautions are maintained until 24 hours after antibiotic initiation.
Hand hygiene is always important but insufficient alone for bacterial meningitis control.
Hub scrubbing reduces CLABSI risk — important but not specific to meningitis transmission.
Biohazard disposal is standard — not specific to meningitis precaution needs.

stem: A patient with bacterial meningitis has BP 110/70 mmHg, HR 110 beats/min, RR 30 breaths/min, SpO2 95%, and temperature 103.5°F. What is the priority nursing action?
opts: Elevate the head of the bed 30 degrees
Keep lights dim at all times
Implement seizure precautions
Maintain bed rest at all times
rat: Bacterial meningitis irritates the meninges and can trigger seizures; the high fever further increases seizure risk. Implementing seizure precautions is the priority to prevent injury.
optRationales: HOB elevation promotes comfort and may reduce cerebral edema but is not the priority given the seizure risk.
Keeping lights dim reduces meningeal irritation from photophobia but is not the immediate priority.
Bacterial meningitis irritates the meninges and the high fever further increases seizure risk — seizure precautions are the priority to prevent injury.
Bed rest is appropriate but secondary to seizure safety.
